title: NIPFP WP 449: Credit Constraints among Unincorporated Enterprises in India (ASUSE 2022-23)
executive_summary: NIPFP Working Paper 449 (Badola & Mukherjee, Jun 2026) uses NSSO's unit-level ASUSE 2022-23 data (458,938 sample enterprises, ~65mn weighted; 57,074 or 7.8mn reporting outstanding loans) to construct a novel three-way credit-constraint classification — Fully, Partially, and Not Constrained — via a multinomial logit model.

Credit access has shifted toward formality: enterprises with formal-source outstanding loans rose from 43.24% (2015-16) to 52.95% (2022-23); informal-only fell to 43.97%. Rural enterprises access formal credit more (57.25%) than urban (46.43%), and rural finances 76.68% of loan value formally vs 65.12% urban.

Manufacturing is credit-starved: it takes only 15.4% of total outstanding loans vs trading 32.16% and services 52.44%; individual manufacturing sub-sectors get <2% of formal credit each, while retail trade (18.67%) and financial services (27.48%) dominate formal loans.

Wide interstate variation: Jharkhand (92.66%), Goa (87.20%), Andhra Pradesh (85.66%) and Odisha (85.05%) source >80% of credit formally, while Rajasthan (48.75%), Madhya Pradesh (51.72%), West Bengal (57.60%) and Bihar (61.30%) lag; all-India formal-only share is 69.89%.

Headline finding: 36.16% of unincorporated enterprises are fully credit-constrained, 28.80% partially, and 35.04% not constrained. Manufacturing is worst hit (53.64% fully constrained) vs trading (40.83%) and services (24.99%, with 55.20% unconstrained).

Multinomial logit (77,744 obs, Pseudo R2 0.26, state fixed effects) confirms enterprise size (turnover and asset value, inverted-U), price-cost margin, GST/VAT registration, bank account, book-keeping, mixed activities and rural location all significantly REDUCE constraint probability.

Marginal effects: manufacturing raises full-constraint probability by 8.1pp; trading raises partial-constraint by 13.3pp; a 1-unit PCM rise cuts full-constraint by 7pp and partial by 10.6pp; having a bank account cuts constraint probability by ~13pp.

Equity gaps persist despite targeted schemes (Mudra, Stand-up India, CGTMSE, ECLGS): female proprietors (+4pp full-constraint) and SC/ST/OBC-owned enterprises face significantly higher credit constraints, signalling weak outreach of financial-inclusion policy.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Manufacturing micro-enterprises are structurally starved of formal credit, taking only 15.4% of outstanding loans despite backward/forward linkage importance, with 53.64% fully credit-constrained.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: For lenders/NBFCs targeting the informal segment, manufacturing borrowers carry higher latent unmet demand but also weaker collateral and turnover profiles — a double-edged origination risk. Track sector-wise share of formal outstanding loans and the fully-constrained ratio (currently 53.64% mfg vs 24.99% services) as a proxy for underserved-market opportunity vs asset-quality risk.

key_takeaways.takeaway: The shift from informal (50.32% in 2015-16) to formal (52.95% in 2022-23) credit sourcing indicates deepening financial inclusion but leaves ~44% still on informal channels, meaning formal-lender penetration into the 65mn-enterprise universe remains shallow.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Formalisation supports better credit histories and repayment visibility, structurally positive for MFI/SFB/fintech lenders' underwriting. Monitor the formal-only enterprise share (currently 52.95%) and rural formal-credit penetration (57.25%) as leading indicators of addressable-market expansion for institutional lenders.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Enterprise size (turnover and asset value) shows an inverted-U relationship with constraint probability, empirically confirming that small, low-asset own-account enterprises are the most credit-rationed segment.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Confirms the classic asymmetric-information thesis: smaller ticket, thinner-file borrowers are the hardest to underwrite and most likely to remain constrained or default. For a lender's small-ticket book, track median borrower turnover and asset value alongside the own-account-enterprise (OAE) mix, which the study flags as significantly more constrained than hired-worker enterprises.

key_takeaways.takeaway: GST/VAT registration, bank-account ownership and formal book-keeping each materially reduce credit-constraint probability, quantifying the value of formalisation and digital financial footprints for underwriting.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: A bank account alone cuts constraint probability by ~13pp and GST registration by 2-7pp — validating GST/UPI/account-aggregator data as high-signal underwriting inputs for thin-file MSME lending. Track share of borrowers that are GST-registered and maintain books of account as a portfolio creditworthiness screen.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Price-cost margin (profitability/market power) is a strong protective factor — a 1-unit rise cuts full-constraint probability by 7pp — echoing Kaplan-Zingales that constrained firms have lower profitability.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Profitability directly signals repayment capacity and reduces perceived default risk; for micro-lending, cash-flow/margin-based assessment outperforms collateral-only models in this segment. Track borrower-level PCM (mean 0.56 in sample) as a repayment-capacity proxy where formal financials are absent.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Female proprietors (+4pp full-constraint) and SC/ST/OBC-owned enterprises face significantly higher credit constraints despite Mudra, Stand-up India, CGTMSE and ECLGS — revealing a persistent policy-outreach gap.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: The 60% SC/ST/OBC and 11% female-proprietor sample shares mean a large borrower base remains under-served, a socio-economic credit-access frictions signal for priority-sector and inclusion-mandated lending. Track disbursement penetration and constraint ratios among women/SC-ST-OBC owners as an early indicator of scheme effectiveness and PSL-target achievability.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Sharp interstate divergence in formal-credit reliance (Jharkhand 92.66% vs Rajasthan 48.75%) implies geography is a first-order determinant of institutional credit access independent of firm fundamentals.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: State-level formal-credit penetration should feed into lenders' geographic risk-weighting and branch-expansion strategy; laggard states (Rajasthan, MP, West Bengal, Bihar) represent both higher informal-competition and higher unmet-demand markets. Track state-wise formal-only outstanding-loan share as an expansion and concentration-risk metric.

entities_impacted.impact: 36.16% fully and 28.80% partially credit-constrained; manufacturing sub-segment most rationed, limiting investment, productivity and growth
Takes only 15.4% of total outstanding loans; 53.64% fully credit-constrained — the most credit-deprived activity despite economic linkage importance
Data source; survey provides the empirical base but limited variables constrain constraint-variable construction and analytical granularity

entities_impacted.entity: Government credit schemes (Mudra, Stand-up India, CGTMSE, ECLGS)
entities_impacted.impact: Despite targeted design, female and SC/ST/OBC enterprises remain more constrained, indicating outreach and implementation gaps
Formal-credit share rose to 52.95% of borrowing enterprises; large untapped, underserved MSME market with GST/account-aggregator data enabling better underwriting
Structural credit gap in 65mn-enterprise informal economy constrains employment (110mn workers) and GDP contribution; wide interstate and gender/caste disparities

learning: Access to credit is not binary: this paper's three-way classification (fully/partially/not constrained) shows that borrowing from informal sources without reporting cost/availability problems is NOT a credit constraint — analysts should avoid equating informal borrowing with financial exclusion or distress.

Non-borrowers are analytically ambiguous and were rightly excluded (377,389 enterprises) — treating zero-outstanding-loan firms as 'unconstrained' would overstate access; a lender's 'no-loan' customers may be self-rationed by anticipated rejection, a hidden pool of latent demand and credit risk.

Firm size shows an inverted-U (not linear) relationship with credit access — the smallest own-account, low-asset, low-turnover enterprises are structurally the most rationed, so segment-level credit models must capture non-linear size effects rather than assume monotonic improvement.

Formalisation signals (GST registration, bank account, book-keeping) are quantifiably strong reducers of credit constraint — for thin-file MSME underwriting these digital/regulatory footprints are high-signal, low-cost substitutes for traditional financial statements and collateral.
